name: 显微镜的使用
subject: biology
curriculum_points: 正确、规范地制作临时装片，使用显微镜进行观察，能够针对观察结果中可能出现的成像不佳等情况，从材料制备、仪器设备、操作程序等方面初步分析原因。
为学生提供多种生物材料，指导学生制作临时装片，利用显微镜进行观察，使学生初步学会使用显微镜观察的方法，形成对细胞结构的感性认识。
实验探究活动: 练习使用光学显微镜; 用显微镜观察池塘水中的微小生物;制作植物细胞、动物细胞的临时装片，用显微镜观察细胞结构。

excerpts.text: 正确、规范地制作临时装片，使用显微镜进行观察，能够针对观察结果中可能出现的成像不佳等情况，从材料制备、仪器设备、操作程序等方面初步分析原因。
为学生提供多种生物材料，指导学生制作临时装片，利用显微镜进行观察，使学生初步学会使用显微镜观察的方法，形成对细胞结构的感性认识。
实验探究活动: 练习使用光学显微镜; 用显微镜观察池塘水中的微小生物;制作植物细胞、动物细胞的临时装片，用显微镜观察细胞结构。

interactive_resources.code_hint: Canvas 生物过程动画：细胞/分子/生态系统等可视化模拟

supplements.curriculum_md_raw:
# 显微镜的使用

## 基本信息
- **学科**: 生物
- **学段**: 初中
- **年级**: 七年级
- **章节**: 细胞基础
- **知识点**: 显微镜的使用
- **课标版本**: 义务教育课程标准（2022年版）

---

## 一、课标原文

### 1.1 内容要求
> 正确、规范地制作临时装片，使用显微镜进行观察，能够针对观察结果中可能出现的成像不佳等情况，从材料制备、仪器设备、操作程序等方面初步分析原因.

### 1.2 学业要求
> 学习本主题后，学生能够正确使用显微镜等工具观察生物.

### 1.3 教学提示
>

---

## 二、教学目标

### 2.1 知识与技能
- 学生能够理解显微镜的使用的基本概念和原理
- 学生能够正确进行相关计算和推理
- 学生能够识别和描述相关的概念、现象或材料
- 学生能够理解显微镜的使用的基本概念和原理

### 2.2 过程与方法
- 学生经历通过自主探究和合作交流，掌握学习生物学的方法
- 学生能够运用生物学学科方法解决问题
- 学生能够在交流中表达自己的思路
- 学生经历通过自主探究和合作交流，掌握学习生物学的方法

### 2.3 情感态度与价值观
- 学生感受生物学与现实生活密切相关
- 学生体验生物学探究的乐趣，树立学好生物学的信心
- 学生养成认真细致、独立思考的学习习惯
- 学生感受生物学与现实生活密切相关

---

## 三、教学重难点

### 3.1 教学重点
- 显微镜的使用的基本内容和基本方法
- 形成完整的知识体系结构
- 显微镜的使用的基本内容和基本方法
- 形成完整的知识体系结构

### 3.2 教学难点
- 掌握显微镜的使用的基本运算和简单应用
- 建立知识之间的联系，实现知识的迁移应用
- 掌握显微镜的使用的基本运算和简单应用
- 建立知识之间的联系，实现知识的迁移应用

## 五、教学建议（学科化补灌）

### 5.1 教学方法
- 以结构与功能、生命过程或生态关系为主线组织「显微镜的使用」教学。
- 通过图示、模型、实验或真实案例，引导学生解释生命现象背后的机制。
- 围绕课标要点“正确、规范地制作临时装片，使用显微镜进行观察，能够针对观察结果中可能出现的成像不佳等情况，从材料制备、仪器设备、操作程序等方面初步分析原因。”，设计观察记录、证据解释和生活应用任务。

supplements.textbook_summary:
## 教材化讲解参考（由课标要点生成）

### 核心概念
显微镜的使用是生物学学习中的重要知识点。教学时应围绕课标要点“正确、规范地制作临时装片，使用显微镜进行观察，能够针对观察结果中可能出现的成像不佳等情况，从材料制备、仪器设备、操作程序等方面初步分析原因。”展开，先明确概念边界，再通过例子、图示、实验、语篇或案例帮助学生建立可迁移的理解。

### 学习路径
1. 情境导入：以结构与功能、生命过程或生态关系为主线组织「显微镜的使用」教学。
2. 探究建构：通过图示、模型、实验或真实案例，引导学生解释生命现象背后的机制。
3. 应用迁移：围绕课标要点“正确、规范地制作临时装片，使用显微镜进行观察，能够针对观察结果中可能出现的成像不佳等情况，从材料制备、仪器设备、操作程序等方面初步分析原因。”，设计观察记录、证据解释和生活应用任务。

### 例题设计方向
可设计一道“识别核心概念”的基础题，一道“解释现象或文本材料”的理解题，一道“迁移到真实情境”的应用题，并要求学生说明依据。

deep_textbook_snippets.text: a. Cytogenetic mapping - stained sections of chromosomes are analyzed using microscope, the distance between genetic markers can be found; Radiation hybrid mapping - breaks DNA using radiation and is affected by recombination frequency; Sequence mapping - DNA sequencing technology used to create physical maps. b. Cytogenetic mapping - stained sections of chromosomes are analyzed using microscope, the approximate distance between genetic markers can be found; Radiation hybrid mapping - breaks DNA using radiation and is unaffected by recombination frequency; Sequence mapping - DNA sequencing technology used to create physical maps. c. Cytogenetic mapping - stained sections of chromosomes are analyzed using microscope, the distance in base pairs between genetic markers can be found; Radiation hybrid mapping - breaks DNA using radiation and is unaffected by recombination frequency; Sequence mapping - DNA sequencing technology used to create physical maps. d. Cytogenetic mapping - stained sections of chromosomes are analyzed using a telescope, the distance between genetic markers can be found; Radiation hybrid mapping - breaks DNA using radiation and is affected by recombination frequency; Sequence mapping - DNA sequencing technology used to create physical maps. 49. How many cells with different genetic variations are possible after a single round of meiosis? a. two b. three c. four d. eight

Determine the Time Spent in Cell-Cycle Stages Problem: How long does a cell spend in interphase compared to each stage of mitosis? Background: A prepared microscope slide of whitefish blastula cross-sections will show cells arrested in various stages of the cell cycle. (Note: It is not visually possible to separate the stages of interphase from each other, but the mitotic stages are readily identifiable.) If 100 cells are examined, the number of cells in each identifiable cellcycle stage will give an estimate of the time it takes for the cell to complete that stage. Problem Statement: Given the events included in all of interphase and those that take place in each stage of mitosis, estimate the length of each stage based on a 24-hour cell cycle. Before proceeding, state your hypothesis. Test your hypothesis: Test your hypothesis by doing the following: 1. Place a fixed and stained microscope slide of whitefish blastula cross-sections under the scanning objective of a light microscope. 2. Locate and focus on one of the sections using the low-power objective of your microscope. Notice that the section is a circle composed of dozens of closely packed individual cells. 3. Switch to the medium-power objective and refocus. With this objective, individual cells are clearly visible, but the chromosomes will still be very small. 4. Switch to the high-power objective and slowly move the slide left to right, and up and down to view all the cells in the section (Figure 10.10). As you scan, you will notice that most of the cells are not undergoing
> **[Figure 236, Page 296]** A wide dia

Determine the Time Spent in Cell Cycle Stages Problem: How long does a cell spend in interphase compared to each stage of mitosis? Background: A prepared microscope slide of blastula cross-sections will show cells arrested in various stages of the cell cycle. It is not visually possible to separate the stages of interphase from each other, but the mitotic stages are readily identifiable. If 100 cells are examined, the number of cells in each identifiable cell cycle stage will give an estimate of the time it takes for the cell to complete that stage. Problem Statement: Given the events included in all of interphase and those that take place in each stage of mitosis, estimate the length of each stage based on a 24-hour cell cycle. Before proceeding, state your hypothesis. Test your hypothesis: Test your hypothesis by doing the following: 1. Place a fixed and stained microscope slide of whitefish blastula cross-sections under the scanning objective of a light microscope. 2. Locate and focus on one of the sections using the scanning objective of your microscope. Notice that the section is a circle composed of dozens of closely packed individual cells. 3. Switch to the low-power objective and refocus. With this objective, individual cells are visible. 4. Switch to the high-power objective and slowly move the slide left to right, and up and down to view all the cells in the section (Figure 10.10). As you scan, you will notice that most of the cells are not undergoing mitosis but are in the interphase period of the cell cycle. Figure 10.10 Slowly scan whitefish blastula cells with

FIGURE 4.2 (a) Most light microscopes in a college biology lab can magnify cells up to approximately 400 times and have a resolution of about 200 nanometers. (b) Electron microscopes provide a much higher magnification, 100,000x, and have a resolution of 50 picometers. (credit a: modification of work by "GcG"/Wikimedia Commons; credit b: modification of work by Evan Bench) Electron Microscopes In contrast to light microscopes, electron microscopes (Figure 4.2b) use a beam of electrons instead of a beam of light. Not only does this allow for higher magnification and, thus, more detail (Figure 4.3), it also provides higher resolving power. The method to prepare the specimen for viewing with an electron microscope kills the specimen. Electrons have short wavelengths (shorter than photons) that move best in a vacuum, so we cannot view living cells with an electron microscope. In a scanning electron microscope, a beam of electrons moves back and forth across a cell’s surface, creating details of cell surface characteristics. In a transmission electron microscope, the electron beam penetrates the cell and provides details of a cell’s internal structures. As you might imagine, electron microscopes are significantly more bulky and expensive than light microscopes. FIGURE 4.3 (a) These Salmonella bacteria appear as tiny purple dots when viewed with a light microscope. (b) This scanning electron microscope micrograph shows Salmonella bacteria (in red) invading human cells (yellow). Even though subfigure (b) shows a different Salmonella specimen than subfigure (a), you can still obser

deep_worked_example.solution_outline: 先描述现象或实验条件，再指出关键概念和证据，最后给出解释或结论。

exercises.stem: 依据课标「显微镜的使用」：正确、规范地制作临时装片，使用显微镜进行观察，能够针对观察结果中可能出现的成像不佳等情况，从材料制备、仪器设备、操作程序等方面初步分析原因。 请简要阐述并举例。
exercises.answer: 应答覆盖课标要点，体现掌握与应用。

exercises.stem: 比较说明：为学生提供多种生物材料，指导学生制作临时装片，利用显微镜进行观察，使学生初步学会使用显微镜观察的方法，形成对细胞结构的感性认识。
exercises.answer: 需写出关键概念、过程或方法，避免只背结论。

real_world: 观察人体健康、植物生长、生态环境或食品生产中与「显微镜的使用」相关的生命现象。
